title: The Measurement Gap: Why Indian D2C Brands Are Flying Blind on Marketing Spend
excerpt: Across the country's fastest-growing consumer brands, a quiet crisis is unfolding in spreadsheets. Marketing budgets are growing. Attribution infrastructure is not keeping pace.
content:
Every Monday morning, somewhere in Mumbai or Bengaluru or Delhi, a brand manager is doing the same thing: opening three separate Excel sheets, copying numbers from agency reports into a master file, and trying to figure out whether the ₹18 lakh spent on influencer campaigns last month actually moved the needle.

This scene, repeated across hundreds of India's direct-to-consumer brands, represents one of the most consequential inefficiencies in the country's marketing economy. Money is moving. But the infrastructure to understand where it goes — and what it does — hasn't kept up.

The scale of the problem

India's D2C sector is now a serious commercial force. By most estimates, it crossed ₹50,000 crore in gross merchandise value in 2023 and continues to expand rapidly, fueled by a generation of founders who understand digital marketing well and are willing to invest in it aggressively.

But aggressive spending and intelligent spending are not the same thing.

The brands getting this wrong aren't unsophisticated. They have CMOs with good instincts and agencies with real capabilities. The problem is structural. Their attribution infrastructure — the systems that connect marketing actions to business outcomes — was built for a simpler era, when a brand ran one or two channels and could loosely track what worked.

Today, the typical funded D2C brand might be running influencer campaigns across fifteen creators, editorial placements in six publications, an affiliate program with twenty partners, performance ads on Google and Meta, and podcast sponsorships on top of that. Each of these channels has its own reporting format, its own definition of a conversion, and its own incentive to present numbers favorably.

How attribution breaks down

The breakdown happens at the seams between channels.

Consider a customer's actual journey. She sees a fitness creator's reel about a protein brand on Tuesday. She doesn't click. Three days later, she sees a review in a health newsletter she subscribes to. She clicks through but doesn't buy. On Saturday, she searches for the brand directly, finds it, and purchases.

In most attribution setups, this sale gets credited entirely to organic search. The influencer gets zero credit. The newsletter gets zero credit. The brand's performance marketing team reports an efficient acquisition. The influencer team reports poor conversion. And nobody in the room has a clear picture of what actually drove the sale.

This isn't a hypothetical edge case. It's the dominant pattern of how Indian consumers actually make considered purchase decisions, particularly in categories like beauty, wellness, and fashion where the purchase cycle is longer and research-intensive.

The agency complexity layer

The measurement problem is compounded by how most Indian brands structure their marketing operations. Unlike large multinationals with integrated in-house teams, mid-sized D2C brands typically work with multiple external agencies simultaneously — one for influencer marketing, one for PR and publications, another for performance advertising, sometimes a fourth for affiliate programs.

Each agency arrives with its own reporting dashboard, its own attribution model, and its own definition of what constitutes success. The influencer agency measures reach and engagement. The PR studio measures placements and estimated media value. The performance agency measures ROAS. None of these metrics are wrong, exactly — but they're not comparable, and they're not unified.

The brand manager in the middle is left to synthesize four incompatible data streams into a coherent picture of marketing ROI. Most of them make do. Some make expensive mistakes.

What the best-run brands are doing differently

A growing cohort of brands — still a minority, but a visible one — has figured out a different approach. They've stopped treating attribution as an agency responsibility and started treating it as core infrastructure that the brand owns.

The mechanics are straightforward: every external partner — influencer, publication, affiliate, agency — gets a unique tracking link. Every campaign runs through the same attribution layer. Commission codes provide a secondary attribution path for customers who screenshot links and buy later. And all of this data feeds into a unified view that the brand controls, not any individual vendor.

The result isn't just cleaner reporting. It changes the entire dynamic of agency relationships. When every partner's performance is measured against the same standard, the conversations stop being about vanity metrics and start being about cost per sale. Agencies that are genuinely delivering have their results validated and their contracts renewed. Agencies that were hiding behind impressive-sounding metrics have nowhere to hide.

The infrastructure moment

India's D2C sector is at an inflection point in marketing sophistication. The brands that will compound their advantages over the next five years are the ones investing now in the infrastructure to understand their marketing spend with precision.

This isn't about having better data for its own sake. It's about making compounding decisions: doubling down on the creator who drives real conversions at acceptable cost, cutting the publication placement that generates traffic but no buyers, identifying the affiliate partner whose audience is your actual customer.

The measurement gap is real. It's expensive. And for a meaningful number of Indian brands, closing it is the highest-leverage marketing investment they could make.
author: The Korant

title: The Creator Economy's Dirty Secret: Most Brand Deals Aren't Tracked
excerpt: India has one of the world's most active influencer marketing markets. It also has one of the weakest attribution cultures. The gap between what brands pay and what they can prove is wider than most will admit.
content:
India's influencer marketing industry is projected to reach ₹3,375 crore by 2026, according to estimates from the Advertising Standards Council of India. That's a large number. What's less discussed is how much of that spend is effectively unaccountable — deployed without the infrastructure to know whether it worked.

Interviews with brand managers, agency founders, and creators across the ecosystem point to a consistent picture: the tracking infrastructure in most influencer campaigns is rudimentary at best, absent at worst. Brands are spending serious money on creator partnerships, but many of them can't tell you, with precision, what those partnerships returned.

What tracking actually looks like in practice

The standard setup for an influencer campaign in India is remarkably low-tech. A creator posts content. The brand monitors likes, comments, shares, saves, and story views. Maybe they give the creator a discount code. At the end of the month, the agency compiles these numbers into a PDF report with some screenshots and an estimated media value calculation.

Estimated media value — the practice of comparing an influencer post's reach to what equivalent advertising space would cost — is perhaps the most widely used and most misleading metric in the industry. It tells you how much the eyeballs would cost on a billboard. It tells you nothing about whether any of those eyeballs belong to people who would ever buy your product.

The brands that have moved past this are running every influencer through a proper tracking architecture: unique redirect links that capture clicks at the individual creator level, discount codes as a backup attribution path, cookie windows long enough to capture the delayed purchase that happens three days after the Instagram story.

This isn't technically complicated. It's not expensive. But it requires someone at the brand to own it, and in many cases, nobody does.

Why agencies aren't solving this

The obvious question is why agencies — which charge significant retainers for influencer management — aren't delivering this infrastructure as a matter of course.

The answer is partly structural and partly incentive-driven. Most influencer agencies are organized around creative and relationship functions: finding the right creators, negotiating deals, managing content approval, ensuring compliance. Their teams are built for this. Building and maintaining a technical attribution layer is a different capability entirely, and most agencies haven't invested in it.

There's also a subtler incentive problem. An agency that provides rigorous per-creator attribution data is an agency that makes it easy to see which of its creator relationships are underperforming. Some agencies, consciously or not, prefer the softer ground of reach and engagement metrics, where everything can be framed positively.

This isn't a criticism of the agency model per se. Agencies provide real value in creator relationships, content strategy, and campaign management. But the measurement function shouldn't live with the agency. It should live with the brand.

The creator perspective

Creators, for their part, have mixed feelings about performance-based measurement. Those who are confident in their ability to drive purchase intent — typically creators with tight, niche audiences and high engagement rates — are often enthusiastic about tracking. They want to prove their value and command higher rates.

Creators with larger but more diffuse audiences are sometimes wary of hard performance metrics, knowing that raw conversion numbers may not capture the brand awareness value they provide. This tension is real and legitimate. Not every creator partnership is optimized for immediate sales conversion, and a sophisticated brand understands the difference.

But even for brand awareness-oriented campaigns, basic tracking — how many people clicked, what was their behavior on site, did they come back later — provides enormously valuable information that most brands are currently leaving on the table.

The market is maturing

There are signs that this is changing. The creator economy has been through an extended period of hype-driven spending, and a correction toward accountability was inevitable. Brands that burned through influencer budgets in 2021 and 2022 without meaningful returns are now asking harder questions.

The brands emerging as leaders in the space are the ones that have built the measurement infrastructure to answer those questions. They know, at the creator level, what their cost per sale looks like. They know which audience demographics convert. They know the difference between a creator who drives clicks and a creator who drives buyers.

That knowledge is becoming a competitive advantage. The brands that have it are making better decisions, faster. The brands that don't are still flying blind — at significant and ongoing cost.
author: The Korant

title: The Quiet Power of Affiliate Programs: How Indian Brands Are Building Sales Teams They Don't Employ
excerpt: A growing number of Indian D2C brands are discovering that their most effective salespeople are customers who were never hired, never trained, and only get paid when they deliver.
content:
Meenakshi runs a food blog from Pune. She has 8,400 Instagram followers, a modestly trafficked website, and a genuine passion for artisanal food products. She also, without particularly trying to build a business around it, earns somewhere between ₹4,000 and ₹12,000 a month recommending products she uses to an audience that trusts her judgment.

She is, in the language of performance marketing, an affiliate. But the founders of the brands she partners with tend to call her something else: an ambassador. The distinction matters more than it sounds.

The structural difference

Traditional affiliate marketing — the kind that emerged from global networks in the early 2000s — is a transactional arrangement. Publishers place links, clicks convert, commissions get paid, and the relationship is mediated entirely through numbers on a dashboard. There's no brand relationship, no content strategy, often no communication beyond automated payout confirmations.

The ambassador model that's gaining traction among Indian D2C brands is structurally different. It centers on building real relationships with people who genuinely use and believe in the product — customers who have enough of a network, whether 500 followers or 50,000, to influence purchase decisions in their community.

The mechanism is similar to traditional affiliate marketing: unique tracking links, discount codes, commission on sales. But the selection, onboarding, and management look more like a customer success function than a media buying one.

Why this works particularly well in India

The cultural logic behind ambassador programs maps onto how purchasing decisions actually get made in India. Research consistently shows that recommendations from trusted personal networks carry more weight here than in most Western markets. The concept of word of mouth isn't a marketing abstraction — it's the primary way that many product categories achieve penetration.

WhatsApp groups, resident welfare association networks, alumni communities, and workplace social circles are the actual channels through which product recommendations travel in Indian middle-class life. A formal ambassador program systematizes and incentivizes behavior that was already happening informally.

When a satisfied customer shares a tracking link in her apartment complex's WhatsApp group and earns ₹200 for every resulting sale, she's doing something she might have done anyway — the program just makes it worth doing more consistently, and gives the brand visibility into what's working.

The operational requirements

Brands that have built successful ambassador programs tend to emphasize a few operational fundamentals that distinguish working programs from ones that launch with fanfare and quietly die.

The first is frictionless onboarding. Programs that require extensive paperwork, manual approval processes, or multi-day waits between application and activation lose momentum. The moment of enthusiasm — when a customer wants to tell her friends about a product she loves — is perishable. A program that captures it in minutes is worth more than a perfectly designed one that takes a week to get started.

The second is dual attribution. Tracking links capture digital clicks. But a significant portion of ambassador-driven sales happen through discount codes shared in voice notes, screenshots, and face-to-face conversations. A program that relies solely on link clicks is systematically undercounting conversions and underpaying ambassadors — creating exactly the wrong incentives.

The third is transparent dashboards. Ambassadors who can see their clicks, conversions, and earnings in real time stay engaged. Opacity, even unintentional opacity caused by delayed reporting, kills programs. Ambassadors who aren't sure whether their efforts are working stop making efforts.

The economics at scale

The financial case for ambassador programs, when they work, is compelling. The effective cost per acquisition is typically commission-only — zero base cost, no minimum guarantees, no wasted spend on creators whose audiences don't convert. The brand pays only for results.

But the real advantage isn't unit economics. It's the compounding nature of the asset. A well-run ambassador program grows the number of credible voices advocating for a brand in an organic, trust-based way. Those advocates are harder for competitors to neutralize than paid advertising placements. They represent relationships with real people who have staked some personal credibility on recommending the product.

A few hundred active ambassadors, each consistently driving a handful of sales per month, add up to a sales channel that costs a fraction of equivalent paid media and converts at a fraction of the cost per acquisition.

The brands that have figured this out are not treating affiliate as a secondary channel or a nice-to-have experiment. They're treating it as core commercial infrastructure.
author: The Korant

title: Editorial Placements as Infrastructure: The Long Game Indian Brands Aren't Playing
excerpt: When a brand gets featured in YourStory or Inc42, most marketers treat it as a PR win. A small number treat it as an asset that will still be working in three years. The difference in outcomes is significant.
content:
In early 2024, a Bengaluru-based skincare brand secured a feature in one of India's most-read business publications. The article ran about 1,200 words, told the founder's story, explained the brand's positioning, and included a link to the product website.

The brand's marketing team logged the placement in their quarterly PR report and moved on. Twelve months later, that article was still driving an average of 340 visitors per month to the brand's website — a total of over 4,000 sessions from a single piece of content that required no ongoing investment to maintain.

Whether the team ever noticed, or whether they understood what they had built, is a different question.

The asset versus expense distinction

Most marketing spend is expense: you pay for reach or attention or conversions, and when you stop paying, the effect stops. Google Ads, Meta campaigns, influencer stories — the economics are broadly similar. The investment buys you outcomes for a defined period, after which the tap runs dry.

Editorial placements in high-authority publications operate differently. A well-placed article in a publication with strong domain authority doesn't just deliver traffic during the week it's published. It continues to rank in search results, generate referral traffic, and pass link equity to the brand's website for months or years afterward. The cost structure looks like an expense on a P&L, but the asset it creates behaves more like a long-lived investment.

This distinction matters enormously for how brands should think about allocating marketing budgets — and for how they should measure the ROI of editorial placements.

What most brands get wrong about measurement

The standard approach to measuring PR and editorial placements is to count placements, estimate reach, and calculate something called earned media value — the cost of equivalent advertising space. This methodology is deeply flawed, but it's what most PR agencies report and what most brand managers have learned to accept.

The problems are obvious once you examine them. Estimated media value says nothing about whether anyone read the article, clicked through to the website, or took any commercial action. It compares apples to oranges: a display ad impression and a thoughtful editorial feature are not equivalent marketing events, regardless of what the media kit says.

Better measurement looks different. It starts with UTM-tagged tracking links in every article, so direct click-through traffic is captured and attributable. It layers in branded search volume analysis — tracking whether organic searches for the brand name increase in the weeks following a major placement. It looks at traffic decay curves to understand which articles continue driving traffic over time and which spike and disappear. And it calculates cost per click against the brand's performance marketing benchmarks, so editorial placements compete for budget on comparable terms.

The SEO compounding effect

Beyond direct traffic, editorial placements in high-domain-authority publications serve a structural SEO function that few brands explicitly plan for but many accidentally benefit from.

Search engine rankings are partly determined by the number and quality of external sites linking to a domain. A link from a publication with genuine editorial credibility and high organic traffic passes meaningful link equity to the brand's website — contributing to its ability to rank for competitive keywords over time.

For a brand in a crowded category like skincare, supplements, or apparel, this accumulated link equity represents a compounding competitive advantage. The brand that has been systematically building editorial coverage for three years has a structural SEO head start over a competitor that spends equivalently on paid media and nothing on editorial. That head start is very difficult to close quickly.

Podcast and video as the underrated category

Within the broader editorial category, podcast appearances and long-form YouTube reviews occupy a specific and undervalued position. They generate less direct traffic than written articles. But the quality of attention they command is substantially different.

A founder who is interviewed for forty-five minutes on a podcast that her target customer listens to weekly has created a very different kind of brand impression than a display ad or a sponsored Instagram post. The sustained attention, the conversational format, and the implied endorsement of a trusted podcast host combine to create purchase intent of a depth that short-form media rarely achieves.

The measurement challenge with podcasts is that the purchase journey is typically longer and more indirect than editorial articles. Listeners often convert days or weeks after hearing an episode, and the path from podcast to purchase usually involves several additional touchpoints. This makes attribution harder, but it doesn't make the value lower — it makes it misunderstood.

Building an editorial strategy

The brands doing this well tend to think in tiers. Flagship placements in top-tier national business publications — YourStory, Inc42, Economic Times, Mint — are pursued a handful of times per year and function primarily as credibility anchors. Category-specific publications and newsletters provide higher audience relevance at lower cost and frequency. Podcasts and YouTube reviews add depth and earned trust in communities where the brand wants presence.

The through line is a shift in mental model: from treating editorial as a PR function that generates reports, to treating it as a marketing infrastructure investment that compounds over time. The brands that make this shift find that their effective cost per acquisition from editorial channels falls quarter over quarter as the asset base grows — a dynamic that paid media, however well optimized, cannot replicate.
author: The Korant

title: Managing Multiple Marketing Agencies: The Coordination Problem Nobody Talks About
excerpt: The typical funded Indian D2C brand works with three to five agencies simultaneously. The data fragmentation this creates is costly, misunderstood, and almost entirely avoidable.
content:
At any given time, Priya's brand has five people she considers her marketing team. None of them are on payroll.

There's the influencer agency in Gurugram that manages creator relationships. The PR studio in Mumbai that handles publications and editorial outreach. The affiliate network coordinator. The Google Ads freelancer. And the Meta specialist her co-founder found through a LinkedIn referral six months ago.

Priya is the founder of a Pune-based personal care brand. She has raised a seed round, has a functioning product and growing sales, and spends about ₹8 lakh a month on marketing. She has never, in two years of operation, had a single dashboard that showed her what all of that money was doing.

Priya's situation is not unusual. It may, in fact, be the modal operating condition for funded Indian D2C brands at the growth stage.

How the multi-agency model evolved

The proliferation of marketing agencies in India over the past decade has been remarkable. Specialized agencies for influencer marketing, performance advertising, PR, affiliate management, and SEO have each developed distinct capabilities, relationships, and methodologies. For brands trying to grow quickly, accessing specialized expertise through agencies is often the right call — building equivalent capabilities in-house would be more expensive and slower.

The problem is what happens when these specialized agencies operate in parallel without a shared measurement infrastructure. Each agency reports in its own format, uses its own attribution methodology, and presents its channel's performance in isolation. The brand ends up with a collection of local reports but no global view.

The attribution conflict problem

The fragmentation is bad enough on its own. But it's compounded by a specific problem that doesn't get discussed enough: when different agencies are all trying to claim credit for the same conversions, their numbers don't just fail to add up — they actively mislead.

A customer sees an influencer's post, clicks through, doesn't buy. She later sees a publication article, returns to the site, still doesn't buy. A week later, she searches the brand name directly and converts. In most multi-agency reporting setups, the performance marketing team claims this as an organic conversion. The influencer agency, which uses a longer attribution window, also claims it. So does the PR agency, if they're tracking referral traffic at all.

The result is that the brand's reported marketing numbers add up to more than 100% of actual conversions. Each agency believes it's performing well. Nobody is lying, exactly — they're each using their own attribution framework. But the brand manager trying to decide where to allocate next month's budget is working with fundamentally misleading data.

What shared infrastructure looks like

The solution is architectural: the brand needs to own its attribution layer, and every agency needs to plug into it rather than running their own parallel measurement systems.

In practice, this means every external partner — whether they're an influencer agency, PR firm, or affiliate manager — operates through the brand's tracking infrastructure. Influencers get unique redirect links generated by the brand's system, not the agency's. Publications receive UTM-tagged links that the brand controls. Affiliates operate through the brand's commission tracking rather than a third-party network.

The agencies can still see their own performance data — in fact, this setup often gives them cleaner data than they had before. But the brand sees everything, on the same scale, using the same attribution methodology. Cross-channel comparisons become meaningful. Budget allocation decisions become defensible.

The agency relationship shift

What's interesting about brands that move to this model is what it does to their agency relationships. The dynamic changes significantly, and mostly for the better.

Agencies that are genuinely performing benefit immediately. Their real results are visible and undeniable. They stop having to argue for credit in situations where other agencies are also claiming it. They can go into budget conversations with confidence.

Agencies that were obscuring poor performance behind favorable metric selection have a harder time. But this is a feature, not a bug. The brands that have invested real money in agency relationships deserve to know which of those relationships are working.

Most agency founders, when they encounter this model, actually prefer it — even if there's initial friction. The clarity it provides makes their jobs easier. It removes the ambiguity about what success looks like. And it creates the conditions for long-term partnerships based on demonstrated results rather than political capital.

The cost of not fixing this

For Priya and the thousands of founders in her position, the cost of operating without unified attribution is real and ongoing. Every month that passes without clarity on which channels are driving sales is a month in which budget is almost certainly being misallocated.

The influencer agency that's driving strong conversions at ₹190 per sale deserves more investment. The PR studio that's generating traffic but no buyers should have its brief renegotiated. The affiliate program that's dramatically outperforming on cost per acquisition should be expanded, not held at the same budget because nobody has noticed how well it's doing.

These decisions can't be made well without the data. And the data won't exist until someone decides the brand should own the measurement layer — not delegate it to agencies whose incentives are, by definition, partial.
author: The Korant
